content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 30, 2016

John McCann
Councilmember
276 Fourth Avenue
Chula Vista, CA 91910

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-16-184

Dear Mr. McCann:

       This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ and Section 1090. We are not a finder of fact when rendering
advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are
complete and accurate.

       As required by statute, we have forwarded your request to the Attorney General's Office and
the San Diego County District Attorney's Office. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a
response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) And the following advice is not admissible in a
criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor. (Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTIONS

       1. Does the Act prohibit you, as a Chula Vista City Councilmember, from taking part in
governmental decisions relating to the proposed project to expand the Sharp Chula Vista Medical
Center (the "hospital expansion project") given that Sharp HealthCare is both the applicant pursuing
the hospital expansion project and your employer?

       2. If the Act prohibits you from taking part in those decisions because you have a
disqualifying conflict of interest, may you appear as a member of the general public with respect to
those decisions?

       3. Does Section 1090 prohibit you from making or participating in the making of, or the
City from entering into, contracts relating to the hospital expansion project?

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-184
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. Yes. The Act prohibits you from taking part in decisions relating to the hospital expansion project because those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your financial interest in Sharp HealthCare as a source of income.

2. No. The Act also prohibits you from appearing as a member of the general public with respect to the decisions at issue because you do not have a "personal interest" in those decisions.

3. Section 1090 prohibits you from making or participating in the making of the potential development agreements relating to the hospital expansion project; however, the City may enter into those agreements pursuant to the remote interest exception set forth in Section 1091(b)(1) so long as (1) you disclose your financial interest in the agreements to the City, (2) your interest is noted in the City's official records, and (3) you abstain from any participation in the making of the agreements.

FACTS

Sharp HealthCare is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization that provides health care services throughout San Diego County. Sharp HealthCare is an umbrella organization with numerous health care groups and providers under its administration, including four acute-care hospitals, three specialty hospitals, two affiliated medical groups, and a full spectrum of other facilities and services. With more than 18,000 employees and 2,600 affiliated physicians, Sharp HealthCare is the largest non-governmental employer in San Diego County.

Located within the City of Chula Vista, Sharp Chula Vista Medical Center ("SCVMC") is one of Sharp HealthCare's four acute-care hospitals. Although the SCVMC has over 343 beds, it currently experiences daily shortages of 25 to 30 beds, resulting in diversions of patients to other facilities. SCVMC's bed shortage is anticipated to increase as the population in and around the City continues to expand. In 2012, Sharp HealthCare began planning to expand the hospital's capacity to meet the increased demand.

Sharp HealthCare recently submitted to the City a proposal to expand the SCVMC (the "hospital expansion project"). Sharp HealthCare proposes to construct a new hospital tower within the area of SCVMC's existing campus. The new hospital tower would be seven stories in height and would include 138 beds, 6 operating rooms, intensive care unit rooms, and other associated hospital facilities. In order to approve the hospital expansion project, the City would have to do all of the following: issue a conditional use permit to Sharp HealthCare for the project; adopt a precise plan for the project; approve the project's design review; and certify the project's Final Environmental Impact Report ("Final EIR"), as required by the California Environmental Quality Act.

You are a Chula Vista City Councilmember, and you are employed by Sharp HealthCare as a Computer Technical Analyst within its Information Systems Department. In that position, you support independent medical practices affiliated with Sharp Community Medical Group. You do not serve in an executive position with Sharp HealthCare, or directly support the Sharp Chula Vista Medical Center's information systems, and your position with Sharp HealthCare is located within the City of San Diego and not the City of Chula Vista.

File No. A-16-184
Page No. 3

ANALYSIS

The Act

Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's interests.
Section 87103 also identifies the financial interests that may give rise to a conflict of interest under
the Act, and of those interests, the facts presented and the decisions at issue implicate the following:

• Any interest in a source of income to the official, including promised income, which aggregates
to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) You have a source-
of-income interest in your employer, Sharp HealthCare.

• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate family,
also known as the "personal financial effects" Rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 18702.5.) An
official always has an interest in his or her own personal finances.

Foreseeability and Materiality

Regulation 18701(a) provides that the financial effect of a decision on an official's financial
interest identified in Section 87103 is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a
named party in, or the subject of, the decision. An interest is the subject of a proceeding if the
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or
other entitlement to, or contract with, the interest. (Ibid.)

Sharp HealthCare is the subject of decisions relating to the proposed hospital expansion
project because those decisions involve the approval or denial of permits and other entitlements
necessary to proceed with the project. Therefore, the effect of the decisions at issue is reasonably
foreseeable.

Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the materiality standards for an official's interest in a source
of income, and Regulation 18702.3(a)(1) provides that, for income received by the official for
goods and services provided in the ordinary course of business, including a salary, the effect is
material if the source is a claimant, applicant, respondent, contracting party, or is otherwise named
or identified as the subject of the proceeding.

Sharp HealthCare is the subject of the proceeding with respect to decisions relating to the
proposed hospital expansion project because it is the applicant pursuing the project. Therefore, the
effect of the decisions at issue is also material.

File No. A-16-184
Page No. 4

Accordingly, the Act prohibits you from taking part in decisions relating to the proposed
hospital expansion project because those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on your employer, Sharp HealthCare.2

Appearance as a Member of the General Public

Regulation 18704(d)(2) provides an exception to the Act's prohibition against an official
making, participating in making, or attempting to use his or her official position to influence a
decision in which the official is financially interested; the exception permits an otherwise
disqualified official to appear before his or her agency in the course of its prescribed governmental
function as a member of the general public on matters solely related to the official's personal
interests.

Under Regulation 18704(d)(2), an official's "personal interests" include, but are not limited
to: (A) real property, or (B) a business entity, that is wholly owned by the official, members of the
official's immediate family, or the official and members of his or her immediate family; or (C) a
business entity over which the official, members of the official's immediate family, or the official
and members of his or her immediate family solely or jointly exercise full direction and control.

The facts presented indicate that you do not have a "personal interest" in the decisions at
issue. Therefore, the Act prohibits you from appearing as a member of the general public with
respect to those decisions. Accordingly, you must do all of the following with respect to those
decisions: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally identify each type of economic
interest that is involved in the decision, as well as certain details identifying each interest, on the
record of the meeting; (2) recuse yourself, and abstain from discussion and/or vote on the item.
(Section 87105; Regulation 18707.) Additionally, you may not contact or
appear before any city official or employee regarding the hospital expansion project as any
communication with a city official or employee regarding the project would be an attempt to use
your position to influence the decision. (Regulation 18704(c)(1).)

Section 1090

Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities,
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with
financial interests, other remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their
agencies. (Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Under Section 1090, "the prohibited
act is the making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest." (People v. Honig
(1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void, regardless of
whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38
Cal.3d 633, 646-649.) When Section 1090 is applicable to one member of a governing body of a
public entity, the prohibition cannot be avoided by having the interested board member abstain; the
entire governing body is precluded from entering into the contract. (Id. at pp. 647-649.)

2 Because we have concluded that a disqualifying conflict of interest exists under the Act with respect to your
financial interest in Sharp HealthCare as a source of income, we do not further analyze your financial interest in your
personal finances.

File No. A-16-184
Page No. 5

We employ a six-step analysis to determine whether a public officer has a disqualifying conflict of interest under Section 1090. Steps one through four are not at issue here: (1) you are subject to Section 1090 because you are a city councilmember; (2) a contract is involved because a development agreement between an agency and a developer is a contract for purposes of Section 1090 (Ansolabehere Advice Letter, No. A-16-029; Malibie Advice Letter, No. A-15-243; 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230 (1995)); (3) you would make or participate in the making of a contract because the City Council will determine whether to issue a conditional use permit, adopt a precise plan, approve a design review, and certify the Final EIR for the project; and (4) you would have a financial interest in contracts relating to the project because you are employed by the project applicant. (See Stockton P. & S. Co. v. Wheeler (1924) 68 Cal.App. 592, 602.)

Therefore, Section 1090 prohibits you from making or participating in the making of the potential development agreements relating to the project.

Step Five: Does either a remote or noninterest exception apply?

The Legislature has created various statutory exceptions to Section 1090's prohibition where the financial interest involved is deemed a "remote interest," as defined in Section 1091, or a "noninterest," as defined in Section 1091.5.

If a "remote interest" is present, the contract may be made if: (1) the officer discloses his or her financial interest in the contract to the public agency; (2) the interest is noted in the public agency's official records; and (3) the officer abstains from any participation in the making of the contract. (Section 1091(a), Brunner Advice Letter, No. A-16-168; 88 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 108 (2005).) If a "noninterest" is present, the contract may be made without the officer's abstention. (City of Vernon v. Central Basin Mun. Water Dist. (1999) 69 Cal.App4th 508, 514-515; 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 159-160 (2001).)

Section 1091(b)(1) sets forth a remote interest exception and provides in pertinent part as follows:

"(b) As used in this article, "remote interest" means any of the following: …

"(1) That of an officer or employee of a nonprofit entity exempt from taxation pursuant to Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code (26 U.S.C. Sec. 501(c)(3)), pursuant to Section 501(c)(5) of the Internal Revenue Code (26 U.S.C. Sec. 501(c)(5)), or a nonprofit corporation."

You are an employee of Sharp HealthCare, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit entity. Therefore, we find that you have a remote interest in the potential development agreements between the City and Sharp HealthCare relating to the project pursuant to Section 1091(b)(1). Because you have a remote interest in the potential development agreements, the City may enter into those agreements only if: (1) you disclose your financial interest in the agreements to the City; (2) your interest is noted in the City's official records; and (3) you abstain from any participation in the making of the agreements. (Section 1091(a); Brunner Advice Letter, No. A-16-168; 88 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 108 (2005).)

File No. A-16-184
Page No. 6

Step Six: Does the rule of necessity apply?

There are no facts suggesting that the "rule of necessity" would apply in the present situation. Therefore, this Section 1090 analysis ends here.

Thus, we conclude that Section 1090 prohibits you from making or participating in the making of potential development agreements relating to the project. However, the City may enter into those agreements pursuant to the remote interest exception set forth in Section 1091(b)(1) so long as (1) you disclose your financial interest in the agreements to the City, (2) your interest is noted in the City's official records, and (3) you abstain from any participation in the making of the agreements.

We also conclude that the Act prohibits you from taking part in decisions relating to the hospital expansion project and from appearing as a member of the general public with respect to those decisions. The Act requires you to recuse yourself from those decisions and prohibits you from making any contacts or appearances before a City official or employee regarding the project.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel
[signature]

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

sections.question:
1. Does the Act prohibit you, as a Chula Vista City Councilmember, from taking part in 
governmental decisions relating to the proposed project to expand the Sharp Chula Vista Medical 
Center (the "hospital expansion project") given that Sharp HealthCare is both the applicant pursuing 
the hospital expansion project and your employer? 
2. If the Act prohibits you from taking part in those decisions because you have a 
disqualifying conflict of interest, may you appear as a member of the general public with respect to 
those decisions? 
3. Does Section l 090 prohibit you from making or participating in the making of, or the 
City from entering into, contracts relating to the hospital expansion project?
sections.conclusion:
1. Yes. The Act prohibits you from taking part in decisions relating to the hospital expansion 
project because those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on 
your financial interest in Sharp HealthCare as a source of income. 
2. No. The Act also prohibits you from appearing as a member of the general public with 
respect to the decisions at issue because you do not have a "personal interest" in those decisions. 
3. Section 1090 prohibits you from making or participating in the making of the potential 
development agreements relating to the hospital expansion project; however, the City may enter 
into those a1:,reements pursuant to the remote interest exception set forth in Section 109 l (b )(1) so 
long as ( l) you disclose your financial interest in the agreements to the City, (2) your interest is 
noted in the City's official records, and (3) you abstain from any participation in the making of the 
agreements.

Sharp HealthCare is a 50l(c)(3) nonprofit organization that provides health care services 
throughout San Diego County. Sharp HealthCare is an umbrella organization with numerous health 
care groups and providers under its administration, including four acute-care hospitals, three 
specialty hospitals, two affiliated medical groups, and a full spectrum of other facilities and

sections.analysis:
The Act  
Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise 
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a 
governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the 
decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's interests. 
Section 87103 also identifies the financial interests that may give rise to a conflict of interest under 
the Act, and of those interests, the facts presented and the decisions at issue implicate the following: 
• Any interest in a source of income to the official, including promised income, which aggregates 
to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) You have a source­
of-income interest in your employer, Sharp HealthCare. 
• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate family, 
also known as the "personal financial effects" Rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 18702.5.) An 
official always has an interest in his or her own personal finances. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Regulation 18701 (a) provides that the financial effect of a decision an official's financial 
interest identified in Section 87103 is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a 
named party in, or the subject of, the decision. An interest is the subject of a proceeding if the 
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or 
other entitlement to, or contract with, the interest. (Ibid.) 
Sharp HealthCare is the subject of decisions relating to the proposed hospital expansion 
project because those decisions involve the approval or denial of permits and other entitlements 
necessary to proceed with the project. Therefore, the effect of the decisions at issue is reasonably 
foreseeable. 
Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the materiality standards for an official's interest in a source 
of income, and Regulation l 8702.3(a)( l) provides that, for income received by the official for 
goods and services provided in the ordinary course of business, including a salary, the effect is 
material if the source is a clamant, applicant, respondent, contracting party, or is otherwise named 
or identified as the subject of the proceeding. 
Sharp HealthCare is the subject of the proceeding with respect to decisions relating to the 
proposed hospital expansion project because it is the applicant pursuing the project. Therefore, the 
effect of the decisions at issue is also material.  
Accordingly, the Act prohibits you from taking part in decisions relating to the proposed 
hospital expansion project because those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material 
financial effect on your employer, Sharp HealthCare.2 
Appearance as a Member of the General Public 
Regulation 18704(d)(2) provides an exception to the Act's prohibition against an official 
making, participating in making, or attempting to use his or her official position to influence a 
decision in which the official is financially interested; the exception permits an otherwise 
disqualified official to appear before his or her agency in the course of its prescribed governmental 
function as a member of the general public on matters solely related to the official's personal 
interests. 
Under Regulation l 8704(d)(2), an official's "personal interests" include, but are not limited 
to: (A) real property, or (B) a business entity, that is wholly owned by the official, members of the 
official's immediate family, or the official and members of his or her immediate family; or (C) a 
business entity over which the official, members of the official's immediate family, or the official 
and members of his or her immediate family solely or jointly exercise full direction and control. 
The facts presented indicate that you do not have a "personal interest" in the decisions at 
issue. Therefore, the Act prohibits you from appearing as a member of the general public with 
respect to those decisions. Accordingly, you must do all of the following with respect to those 
decisions: ( 1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally identify each type of economic 
interest that is involved in the decision, as well as certain details identifying each interest, on the 
record of the meeting; (2) recuse yourself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion 
and/or vote on the item. (Section 87105; Regulation 18707.) Additionally, you may not contact or 
appear before any city official or employee regarding the hospital expansion project as any 
communication with a city official or employee regarding the project would be an attempt to use 
your position to influence the decision. (Regulation l 8704(c)( I).) 
Section I 090 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. (Stigall v. City of Taft ( 1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Under Section 1090, "the prohibited 
act is the making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest." (People l'. Honig 
(1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void, regardless of 
whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 
Cal.3d 633, 646-649.) When Section 1090 is applicable to one member of a governing body of a 
public entity, the prohibition cannot be avoided by having the interested board member abstain; the 
entire governing body is precluded from entering into the contract. (Id. at pp. 647-649.) 
8 Because we have concluded that a disqualifying conflict of interest exists under the Act with respect to your 
financial interest in Sharp HealthCare as a source of income, we do not further analyze your financial interest in your 
personal finances.  
We employ a six-step analysis to detennine whether a public officer has a disqualifying 
conflict of interest under Section l 090. Steps one through four are not at issue here: ( 1) you are 
subject to Section 1090 because you are a city councilmember; (2) a contract is involved because a 
development agreement between an agency and a developer is a contract for purposes of Section 
l 090 (Ansolabehere Advice Letter, No. A-16-029; Maltbie Advice Letter, No. A-15-243; 78 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230 ( 1995)); (3) you would make or participate in the making of a contract 
because the City Council will detennine whether to issue a conditional use pennit, adopt a precise 
plan, approve a design review, and certify the Final EIR for the project; and (4) you would have a 
financial interest in contracts relating to the project because you are employed by the project 
applicant. (See Stockton P. & S. Co. v. Wheeler ( 1924) 68 Cal.App. 592, 602.) 
Therefore, Section 1090 prohibits you from making or participating in the making of the 
potential development agreements relating to the project. 
Step Five: Does either a remote or noninterest exception apply? 
The Legislature has created various statutory exceptions to Section 1090's prohibition where 
the financial interest involved is deemed a "remote interest," as defined in Section 1091, or a 
"noninterest," as defined in Section I 091.5. 
If a "remote interest" is present, the contract may be made if: (I) the officer discloses his or 
her financial interest in the contract to the public agency; (2) the interest is noted in the public 
agency's official records; and (3) the officer abstains from any participation in the making of the 
contract. (Section 109l(a); Brunner Advice Letter, No. A-16-168; 88 Ops.CalAtty.Gen. 106, 108 
(2005).) If a "noninterest" is present, the contract may be made without the officer's abstention. 
( Cizv of Vernon v. Celllral Basin Mttn. Water Dist. ( 1999) 69.Cal.App4th 508, 514-515; 84 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 159-160 (200 I).) 
Section 1091 (b )( l) sets forth a remote interest exception and provides in pertinent part as 
follows: 
"(b) As used in this article, "remote interest" means any of the following: ... 
"( 1) That of an officer or employee of a nonprofit entity exempt from 
taxation pursuant to Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code (26 
U.S.C. Sec. 501(c)(3)), pursuant to Section 50l(c)(5) of the Internal Revenue 
Code (26 U.S.C. Sec. 50I(c)(5)), or a nonprofit corporation." 
You are an employee of Sharp HealthCare, a 50l(c)(3) nonprofit entity. Therefore, we find 
that you have a remote interest in the potential development agreements between the City and Sharp 
HealthCare relating to the project pursuant to Section 1091(b)(l). Because you have a remote 
interest in the potential development agreements, the City may enter into those agreements only if: 
( l) you disclose your financial interest in the agreements to the City; (2) your interest is noted in the 
City's official records; and (3) you abstain from any participation in the making of the agreements. 
(Section 109l(a); Brunner Advice Letter, No. A-16-168; 88 Ops.CalAtty.Gen. 106, 108 (2005).) 


Step Six: Does the rule of necessity apply?  
There are no facts suggesting that the "rule of necessity" would apply in the present 
situation. Therefore, this Section 1090 analysis ends here. 
Thus, we conclude that Section 1090 prohibits you from making or participating in the 
making of potential development agreements relating to the project. However, the City may enter 
into those agreements pursuant to the remote interest exception set forth in Section 1091 (b )( 1) so 
long as ( 1) you disclose your financial interest in the agreements to the City, (2) your interest is 
noted in the City's official records, and (3) you abstain from any participation in the making of the 
agreements. 
We also conclude that the Act prohibits you from taking part in decisions relating to the 
hospital expansion project and from appearing as a member of the general public with respect to 
those decisions. The Act requires you to recuse yourself from those decisions and prohibits you 
from making any contacts or appearances before a City official or employee regarding the project.

agreements.

Section 1090 prohibits you from making or participating in the making of the potential development agreements relating to the hospital expansion project; however, the City may enter into those agreements pursuant to the remote interest exception set forth in Section 1091(b)(1) so long as (1) you disclose your financial interest in the agreements to the City, (2) your interest is noted in the City's official records, and (3) you abstain from any participation in the making of the agreements.
